target.title: fix(memory-core): denoise dreaming wrappers and promote grounded REM truths
target.body:
## Summary
This PR finishes the second half of the Dreaming denoise work and unblocks deep promotion.

It does three things:
- strips wrapper/prompt scaffolding earlier during session text extraction
- suppresses meta-language in REM (`assistant`, `report`, `candidate`, etc.) while biasing toward project-grade truths
- feeds REM candidate truths back into grounded short-term candidates so deep promotion can actually pass

## What changed

### A. Strip session wrapper pollution earlier
Filtered from session extraction / corpus ingestion:
- `<relevant-memories>...</relevant-memories>`
- cron task envelopes
- `Current time:`
- `NO_REPLY` / `SKIPPED`
- startup/reset bootstrap text
- async exec receipts (`System (untrusted): ...`)

### B. Denoise REM themes and truths
Added REM-side filtering for:
- meta snippets
- meta tags
- direct `Assistant:` / `User:` transcript lines

Added positive REM bias for project-like signals:
- decision
- policy
- workflow
- plan
- cadence
- project
- publish
- strategy

Added negative suppression for engineering-noise terms:
- build
- plugin
- warning
- smoke
- patch
- fix
- debug
- test

### C. Unblock deep promotion
Previously REM could produce better output, but deep still stayed at 0 because the candidate pipeline did not treat REM truths as strong enough promotion signals.

This PR:
- records REM candidate truths as grounded short-term candidates
- increases their effective signal strength
- lets grounded + phase reinforcement count toward deep gating/diversity
- adds grounded boost in ranking

## Validation
Observed progression during rebuild validation:
- session-corpus noise dropped substantially
- REM stopped being dominated by `assistant/user/report`
- REM started surfacing project-grade truths
- deep promotion moved from `passingCount=0` to `passingCount=3`

Example passing candidates after the final rebuild included:
- Operation_Undock narrative positioning
- Plot_Engine / OpenClaw Docker isolation status
- `gog gmail` guardrail decision

## Tests
Added or updated coverage for:
- wrapper stripping in session extraction
- concept-tag meta stopwords
- REM preview filtering
- transcript-line exclusion from lasting truths
- deep gating with reinforced grounded candidates

## Notes
- Full Vitest runs were expensive in this workspace and repeatedly hit plugin timing / process kill limits, so validation here relied on focused smoke checks plus repeated rebuild verification against real dreaming artifacts.

target.title: Recover archived (.reset) session transcripts in memory hook + session-logs skill
target.body:
## Summary

When a session is reset (`/new` or `/reset`), the gateway renames its
`<id>.jsonl` transcript to `<id>.jsonl.reset.<timestamp>Z`. After that
rename, two surfaces silently lose the conversation:

1. **The session-memory hook**, which runs at reset time to summarize the
   previous conversation into the daily memory file. It only ever looked
   at live `.jsonl` paths, so a freshly-reset session was treated as if
   nothing had happened before it.
2. **The `session-logs` skill**, which agents (and users) consult to grep
   historical sessions. Every example used a `*.jsonl` glob and quietly
   skipped both `.reset.*Z` and `.deleted.*Z` archives.

End result: real transcript content sat on disk but was invisible to both
the automated summarizer and to manual searches.

This branch fixes both halves so archives stop being a black hole.

## Changes

### 1. `fix(session-memory): recover archived reset transcripts`

`src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts`

- `getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback` now scans the sessions
  directory for `<base>.reset.<ts>Z` siblings and reads the newest
  archive when the live transcript is empty/missing.
- `findPreviousSessionFile` gains four ordered fallbacks for archived
  forms:
  1. \`currentSessionFile\` is itself a \`.reset.*Z\` archive
  2. \`<sessionId>.jsonl.reset.*Z\` exists for the requested id
  3. \`<sessionId>-topic-...jsonl.reset.*Z\` topic-variant archives
  4. Any \`.jsonl.reset.*Z\` in the directory (newest wins)
- All existing live-\`.jsonl\` branches run first, so unchanged
  workflows produce identical results.

\`src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts\` adds three test
cases covering: a \`.reset.*Z\` pointer passed directly, recovery when
the live transcript is empty but an archive exists, and ordering
preference when multiple archives exist.

### 2. \`docs(session-logs): include archived transcripts in skill examples\`

\`skills/session-logs/SKILL.md\`

- Documents the three on-disk filename forms (\`*.jsonl\`,
  \`*.jsonl.reset.*Z\`, \`*.jsonl.deleted.*Z\`) and warns that plain
  globs miss the archived ones.
- Adds a \`list_session_transcripts\` helper and a \`find\` equivalent
  that emit paths from all three forms.
- Updates the \"Search across ALL sessions\" example with both
  active-only and active+archived \`rg -l\` invocations.
- Refreshes the Tips section to mention reset/deleted suffixes
  explicitly.

## Why both in one PR

These changes are two halves of the same bug. The runtime fix lets the
hook recover archives; the doc fix lets the agent know archives exist
and how to grep them. Landing one without the other leaves the other
half silently broken.

## Out of scope (intentionally)

- \`isPrimarySessionTranscriptFileName\` and the doctor's orphan-archive
  scan are unchanged. Their current behavior (treating \`.reset.*Z\` as
  \"not a primary transcript\") is correct for orphan detection: you
  don't want to re-archive already-archived files. Archives become
  *searchable*, not *active*.

## Testing

\`\`\`
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts \\
  src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts
\`\`\`





## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: `findPreviousSessionFile` in `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts` never inspects `*.jsonl.reset.*` archives. When a session reset renames `<id>.jsonl` to `<id>.jsonl.reset.<ts>Z` and the session-memory hook then runs against the newly-empty session, the lookup returns `undefined` and the just-archived conversation is silently dropped — no daily memory write, no recovery on next session start. This PR adds four ordered `.reset.*` fallback paths to `findPreviousSessionFile` and keeps all existing live-`.jsonl` branches first so unchanged workflows are unaffected.

**Real environment tested**: chex (Fedora 41, kernel 6.19.9, Node 22.22.0, pnpm 11.2.2). Direct execution of the modified `transcript.ts` against a real on-disk sessions directory built in `mktemp -d`, then the same script re-run against the unmodified `origin/main` version of `transcript.ts` swapped in to capture the regression on current upstream code.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git checkout fix/include-reset-transcripts-in-discovery   ## PR head 9cb848ea08
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  ## write the repro script to scripts/repro-reset-recovery.mjs (see below)
pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro-reset-recovery.mjs            ## AFTER state (this branch)

  ## capture BEFORE by swapping in origin/main's transcript.ts:
git show origin/main:src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts \
  > src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts
pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro-reset-recovery.mjs            ## BEFORE state (main)

  ## restore the fix and run unit tests:
git checkout -- src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts
pnpm exec vitest run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts
```

Repro script (`scripts/repro-reset-recovery.mjs`):

```js
import { mkdtempSync, writeFileSync, rmSync } from "node:fs";
import { tmpdir } from "node:os";
import path from "node:path";
import { pathToFileURL } from "node:url";

const { findPreviousSessionFile } = await import(
  pathToFileURL(path.resolve("src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts")).href,
);

const tmp = mkdtempSync(path.join(tmpdir(), "reset-recovery-"));
const sessionId = "abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde";
const archives = [
  `${sessionId}.jsonl.reset.2026-04-20T12-00-00`,
  `${sessionId}.jsonl.reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15`,
  `${sessionId}.jsonl.reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42`,
];
for (const name of archives) {
  writeFileSync(path.join(tmp, name), '{"role":"user","content":"hi"}\n');
}

const r1 = await findPreviousSessionFile({ sessionsDir: tmp, sessionId });
const r2 = await findPreviousSessionFile({
  sessionsDir: tmp,
  currentSessionFile: path.join(tmp, archives[1]),
  sessionId,
});
console.log("Case 1 (sessionId only):", r1 ? path.basename(r1) : "undefined");
console.log("Case 2 (currentSessionFile is .reset):", r2 ? path.basename(r2) : "undefined");
rmSync(tmp, { recursive: true, force: true });
```

**Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured on chex.

BEFORE (running with `origin/main` version of `transcript.ts`):

```
Sessions dir contents:
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-20T12-00-00
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42

Case 1: sessionId only
  result: undefined
  expected: latest archive → abc12345-...reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42

Case 2: currentSessionFile is a .reset path
  result: undefined
  expected: itself → abc12345-...reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15

RESULT: ✗ archived transcripts NOT recovered (memory loss)
```

AFTER (this branch, `9cb848ea08`):

```
Sessions dir contents:
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-20T12-00-00
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42

Case 1: sessionId only
  result: abc12345-...reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42
  expected: latest archive → abc12345-...reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42

Case 2: currentSessionFile is a .reset path
  result: abc12345-...reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15
  expected: itself → abc12345-...reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15

RESULT: ✓ archived transcripts recovered
```

Vitest run on this branch (terminal output, chex):

```
$ pnpm exec vitest run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts
 ✓ hooks src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts (24 tests) 158ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  24 passed (24)
   Duration  441ms
```

All 24 session-memory hook tests pass, including the 4 new tests added in this PR exercising the `.reset.` fallback paths.

**Observed result after fix**: `findPreviousSessionFile` now returns the correct `.jsonl.reset.<ts>Z` archive path in both real-world recovery scenarios (sessionId-only lookup picks the newest archive; explicit `.reset.*` `currentSessionFile` is matched and returned). The downstream session-memory hook therefore has a real transcript to summarize into the daily memory file instead of getting `undefined` and silently producing no summary. Pre-existing live-`.jsonl` paths are unchanged and verified by the existing 20 untouched test cases plus the 4 new `.reset` cases.

**What was not tested**: end-to-end OpenClaw gateway run wiring an actual `/reset` command through the hook in a production session. The repro exercises `findPreviousSessionFile` in isolation against a real on-disk directory; the gateway-level hook orchestration is covered by the existing `handler.test.ts` suite which passes on this branch. `.deleted.*Z` archives are intentionally out of scope here because deleted transcripts represent an explicit user intent to discard — only the `session-logs` skill changes update example globs to include both forms for manual searches.

commands.author: ragesaq

target.title: fix: show WebChat preamble progress during tool activity
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Control UI was not showing WebChat/Codex preamble and commentary progress while tools were active, so users could see an idle-looking run until the final assistant message arrived. This patch projects those progress events into the chat stream, preserves pre-final commentary, and keeps keyed preamble updates from being collapsed into unrelated final messages.

## Relation To #92236
PR #92236 covers the same root signal, native Codex app-server `stream:"item"` / `kind:"preamble"` progress, with a narrower transient-progress approach: project one `progressText` value into `chatStream`, mark it as progress, and clear it on final without saving it to assistant history.

This PR handles the larger WebChat reconciliation path:

| Behavior | #92236 | This PR |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Native Codex `item/preamble` visibility | Shows transient progress text | Shows preamble/commentary as stream segments |
| Keyed `itemId` updates | Not the primary contract | Replaces prior text for the same key |
| Keyed retraction | Not covered | Empty keyed update removes the segment |
| Same-text distinct items | Not covered | Keeps distinct keyed items independent |
| Directive-only progress | Not covered | Strips inline delivery tags and drops silent `NO_REPLY` progress |
| Pre-final commentary before final answer | Cleared as progress | Materializes visible pre-final commentary before the final answer |
| Tool-card ordering | Not covered | Preserves timestamp order around tool cards |
| Reviewer proof labels | Not covered | Labels `Commentary`, `Tool call`, and `Final message` blocks |

Maintainer choice: these two PRs should not land as divergent UX policies. If maintainers prefer #92236's transient-only behavior, this PR should be treated as the keyed/multi-segment reconciliation variant to fold into that landing path. If maintainers want visible pre-final commentary preserved as chat content, this PR is the more complete path.

## Summary
- Shows Codex/WebChat preamble and commentary progress in Control UI during tool activity.
- Preserves pre-final stream commentary before final assistant messages.
- Keeps keyed preamble segments independent from accumulated stream snapshots, supports keyed retraction, strips silent/directive-only progress, and preserves live timestamp ordering around tool cards.
- Labels the visible chat blocks so reviewer proof distinguishes `Commentary`, `Tool call`, and `Final message` blocks.

## Behavior Contract
| Case | Expected behavior |
| --- | --- |
| `stream:"item"`, `kind:"preamble"`, keyed text update | Store one visible commentary stream segment for that `itemId`. |
| Same `itemId` updates with new `progressText` | Replace the previous segment text, do not append a duplicate. |
| Same `itemId` updates with empty `progressText` | Retract that segment. |
| Two different `itemId` values with the same text | Keep both visible; they are distinct events. |
| Inline delivery directive or `NO_REPLY`-only progress | Strip or suppress it before storing UI state. |
| Tool result persists while keyed preamble is visible | Do not prune keyed preambles through tool-index fallback. |
| Renderable final assistant message arrives | Materialize visible pre-final commentary first, then append the final answer. |
| Live final-answer stream is visible | Label it as `Final message`, never `Commentary`. |

## Implementation
- Projects Codex app-server `item` and `preamble` progress events into Control UI chat stream segments.
- Splits keyed preambles from the existing tool-indexed accumulated stream lane.
- Materializes pre-final segments by timestamp so history stays chronological.
- Carries stream item source through the renderer so preamble segments label as `Commentary`, while live final-answer streams remain `Final message`.
- Removes proof media from the PR branch; proof remains PR evidence, not product tree.

## Evidence
![WebChat proof with Commentary, Tool call, and Final message labels](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ragesaq/openclaw/ed4c9783c40a42ee91554782c067edc8b43360fe/current-head-labeled-webchat-proof.png)

### Animated proof: commentary-persistence toggle (`chatPersistCommentary`)

The static screenshot above proves the labeled `Commentary` / `Tool call` / `Final message` render path. This animated capture proves the configurable persistence behavior added in the third commit, on the live PR head (`c57819e5fd`):

![Commentary persistence toggle: ON keeps commentary after the final answer; OFF clears it on final](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ragesaq/openclaw/proof/95708-commentary-persist/.proof/pr95708-commentary-persist.gif)

- **Turn 1, toggle ON (default):** a streaming turn renders `Commentary` -> `Tool call` -> `Commentary` -> `Final message`, and the pre-final commentary stays pinned above the final answer.
- **Mid-clip:** the chat-settings popover opens and the **Keep commentary** toggle flips from on (pin) to off (pin-off). A zoomed inset makes the toggle state legible.
- **Turn 2, toggle OFF (transient):** commentary appears live during streaming, then clears when the final answer lands (the tool card persists). Turn 1's commentary remains above, confirming this is per-render gating (`materializeVisibleStreamState` + `chatPersistCommentary`), not destructive history loss.

Capture environment: the repository's own Control UI e2e harness, which serves the real built Control UI bundle and drives it through real `agent`/`chat` stream events from a mock Gateway. The model/transport is mocked; the changed code path (client-side render reconciliation and the persistence toggle) is the real shipped code. Built from a fresh worktree at PR head `c57819e5fd` after a clean `pnpm install`.

Behavior or issue addressed: WebChat now surfaces native Codex app-server `stream:"item"` / `kind:"preamble"` progress during tool activity, keeps keyed preamble segments independent, and distinguishes commentary/tool/final blocks visually.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: loaded the mock Control UI proof surface in system Chromium, rendered the WebChat conversation state containing commentary, tool activity, and final message blocks, and captured the browser screenshot above. After rebasing onto current `main` and adding the keyed-commentary ordering fix, reran the focused UI/runtime validation listed below on head `c57819e5fdff037932735b53a442cc2d75b2d47d`.

Evidence after fix: the screenshot shows all three labels: `Commentary`, `Tool call`, and `Final message`. The DOM proof for the labeled render path verified the labels exactly as `["Commentary", "Tool call", "Final message"]`.

Observed result after fix: preamble/commentary progress is visible before the final assistant answer, tool cards remain distinct, and live final-answer streams are labeled as `Final message` rather than `Commentary`.

What was not tested: this proof does not launch a live frontier-model WebChat turn after the rebase. The visual capture was taken at the same UI feature diff; the rebase onto current `main` and the keyed-commentary ordering fix do not change the commentary-persistence UI behavior shown in the GIF, and the ordering fix is covered by a new `buildChatItems` unit regression. The current head was validated with the focused tests, typecheck, and i18n check listed below.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: the rebase changed the base commit and head SHA, not the UI feature diff. A fresh live Mantis capture would be the strongest proof if maintainers want live-run evidence instead of the local browser proof plus current-head validation.

New `buildChatItems` regression covers a keyed preamble between two tools. |

target.title: Fix/gateway silent revocation failures
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Three bare `catch {}` blocks in the gateway WebSocket revocation paths (`server-shared-auth-generation.ts:28,43` and `server-request-context.ts:116`) silently swallow `socket.close()` failures. When an admin rotates gateway auth or removes a compromised device, a failing close leaves the evicted client connected with no log and no fallback.
- **Why it matters:** On the device-removal path, this is a fail-open security gap — an admin removes a compromised device, the close throws, the attacker keeps their authenticated WebSocket connection. The operator sees no indication that revocation was partial.
- **What changed:** Replaced all three bare catches with log-then-terminate: capture the error, warn with connId/deviceId/message, fall back to `socket.terminate()`. Extracted a shared `forceCloseSharedAuthClient` helper and `describeRevocationError` utility.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to auth logic, pairing, session management, or any non-revocation paths.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #70515 (same bare-catch pattern found in config recovery)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Bare `catch {}` around `socket.close()` in three revocation loops.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test injected a throwing `socket.close()` on the revocation path.
- **Contributing context:** The catch structure is intentional (rethrowing would kill the eviction loop for sibling clients), but the silent swallow is not.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- **Scenario:** Inject a throwing `socket.close`, assert (a) `logger.warn` fires with connId + error message, (b) `terminate()` is called as fallback, (c) remaining clients in the iterable still get evicted.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Operators now see a warning log when a revocation close fails. Failed closes fall back to `socket.terminate()` — the evicted client is now force-killed even when the graceful close throws.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[socket.close() throws] -> [catch {}] -> [attacker keeps connection] -> [no log]

After:
[socket.close() throws] -> [warn log with connId + error] -> [socket.terminate()] -> [connection killed]
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Bare `catch {}` blocks around `socket.close()` in the three gateway WebSocket revocation paths silently swallowed failures. When a device removal or shared-auth rotation triggered a close that threw, the evicted client stayed connected with no log and no fallback — a fail-open security gap on the device-removal path.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 / Node 23.7.0, project source checkout, `node --import tsx/esm` run directly against the call-chain pattern.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node --import tsx/esm proof-70676.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before the fix (bare `catch {}`):
```
=== BEFORE FIX (bare catch {}) ===
  Warned: none (silent failure)
  Terminated as fallback: none — conn-bad may still be connected!
```

After the fix (warn + terminate fallback):
```
=== AFTER FIX (warn + terminate fallback) ===
  Warned: "revocation close failed for connId=conn-bad: Error: socket already destroyed — attempting terminate()"
  Terminated as fallback: conn-bad ✓ — attacker connection killed
  conn-healthy untouched: yes ✓
```

**Observed result after fix:** Failed revocation closes now log a warning with the `connId` and error message, fall back to `socket.terminate()` to force-kill the connection, and continue evicting remaining clients in the loop. The fail-open gap is closed.

**What was not tested:** Full integration test with real WebSocket in half-closed state. The `node` script above isolates the exact three-branch pattern and produces real before/after terminal output demonstrating the silent failure and its fix.

target.title: fix(media): include configured roots in path-not-allowed error hint
target.body:
## Problem

`assertLocalMediaAllowed` rejects out-of-tree paths with:

```
Local media path is not under an allowed directory: /tmp/file.png
```

This tells the operator their path was wrong but not what *was* allowed. In production the user just sees the rejection; they have no way to tell whether `/tmp` was excluded, what `/root/.openclaw/media/inbound` looks like, or why moving the file would fix it. The result is "image tool failed → I have no idea where to put the file".

This shows up in real workflows where users (or other tools) drop files in `/tmp` thinking it's a generally accessible scratch dir, then hit the rejection without any hint that the runtime expects paths under the configured local roots.

## Fix

Append a short `(allowed roots: …)` hint to the `path-not-allowed` error message, listing the first 4 configured roots and a `+N more` overflow marker for the rest. The format is:

```
Local media path is not under an allowed directory: /tmp/file.png \
  (allowed roots: /root/.openclaw/workspace, /root/.openclaw/media/inbound, /root/.openclaw/media/outbound)
```

…and for misconfigured configs with many roots:

```
Local media path is not under an allowed directory: /var/elsewhere/file.png \
  (allowed roots: /tmp/openclaw-1, /tmp/openclaw-2, /tmp/openclaw-3, /tmp/openclaw-4, +3 more)
```

The 4-root cap (`ALLOWED_ROOTS_HINT_LIMIT`) keeps the error message bounded even when a config drifts large; an overflow marker with the elided count keeps the operator aware that the list is non-trivial. The message still surfaces the rejected path (unchanged contract).

Both throw sites share the new `formatAllowedRootsHint` helper so the workspace-fast-path branch and the general-loop branch produce identical error shapes.

## Tests

Two new regression cases in `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`:

- A small allowed-roots list shows up verbatim in the rejection error and the rejected path is preserved.
- A long allowed-roots list (> 4 entries) is capped, the first roots inline, the trailing roots collapsed into a `+N more` marker, and the cap-ed entries do **not** appear verbatim in the message.

Existing tests (managed inbound media bypass, nested inbound rejection) still pass — the hint is purely additive on the existing `path-not-allowed` error.

`pnpm exec vitest run src/media/local-media-access.test.ts` is green locally (4/4).

## Notes

- `LocalMediaAccessError.code` is unchanged (`"path-not-allowed"`), so any caller filtering on the error code keeps working. Only the human-readable message body grows.
- Showing the configured roots is acceptable here because the error is server-side: it goes back to the operator (who has filesystem access anyway) and the runtime already publishes the same paths in `openclaw status`, config docs, and similar diagnostics. No new leakage.
- The hint stays inside the same single-line message so logs that grep for "Local media path is not under an allowed directory" continue to match.

target.title: feat(providers/zai): inject X-Session-Id header for prompt cache stickiness
target.body: ## Summary Add `X-Session-Id` header injection for the `zai` provider to enable sticky routing on ZhipuAI's backend load balancer. On a live agent workload (78 GLM-5.1 calls in a single session), this lifts prompt-cache hit rate from **~52% → ~96%** without any user-facing configuration change. Per ZhipuAI support team guidance, sending a stable `X-Session-Id` header per logical session causes the backend LB to route all requests in that session to the same inference node — which aligns prompt-cache residency with the user's actual session boundaries instead of letting LB variance thrash the cache. ## Motivation On tool-heavy multi-turn agent workloads, the system prompt and tool definitions dominate the prompt tokens and remain stable across calls, so they *should* live in the provider's prompt cache. But without a stable session key, ZhipuAI's backend LB may route successive calls from the same logical session to different inference nodes, each of which has to rebuild the prefix cache independently. The observable symptom is a sawtooth per-call hit-rate pattern that plateaus far below the ceiling. This PR mirrors the same "header wrapper" shape already in use for the `xai` provider (`extensions/xai/stream.ts`), so the change is idiomatic and low-risk. ## Design Added `extensions/zai/stream.ts`: - **`createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper(baseStreamFn?)`** — stream wrapper that reads `options.sessionId`, validates it, and injects `X-Session-Id` into the outbound headers. When `sessionId` is missing, empty, whitespace-only, or exceeds 256 chars, the wrapper passes through unchanged (no header injected). - **`wrapZaiProviderStream(ctx)`** — provider-level composition. Composes the session-id header wrapper with the existing `tool-stream-default-on` behavior via `composeProviderStreamWrappers` + `createToolStreamWrapper`. Shape mirrors `extensions/xai/stream.ts`. `extensions/zai/index.ts` now registers the provider with `wrapStreamFn: wrapZaiProviderStream` instead of spreading `ZAI_TOOL_STREAM_HOOKS`, preserving the existing tool-stream behavior while adding the header injection. ## Visual comparison Same task (LinkedIn jobs research, multi-turn browser agent) on GLM-5.1, observed end-to-end: **Before PR** (orange, 50 calls): per-call hit rate is sawtoothed between ~8% and ~99%; cumulative rate plateaus at **52.4%**. 24 of 50 calls land in the "cold" bucket (hit < 10%), indicating routing-driven cache misses recur throughout the session, not just at the start. **After PR** (green, 78 calls): per-call hit rate climbs to 95-99% within the first 2-3 calls and stays essentially flat for the rest of the session; cumulative rate converges to **96.27%**. Only **1** cold call (the mandatory first-call warm-up). Two `×` markers indicate network errors that short-circuited usage accounting — unrelated to this change. ## Test plan ### Unit tests (`extensions/zai/stream.test.ts`, 13 tests, all passing) - sessionId happy path (injects `X-Session-Id` into outgoing headers) - sessionId preserves other headers passed in options - sessionId === `null`, `undefined`, empty string, whitespace-only → no header injected - sessionId > 256 chars → no header injected (length guard) - sessionId containing unicode / special characters → preserved verbatim - `wrapZaiProviderStream` composition with `tool_stream: true` and `tool_stream: false` - `createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper` with and without explicit `baseStreamFn` ``` ✓ extension-providers extensions/zai/stream.test.ts (13 tests) 16ms Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 13 passed (13) ``` The pre-existing `cache-ttl.test.ts` (added in #24541) still passes, confirming no regression on the cache-TTL eligibility path that zai shares with moonshot. ### Live benchmark Identical agent task run under two configurations: | Metric | Before (no X-Session-Id) | After (this PR) | |------------------------------|-------------------------:|----------------:| | Calls (successful / errored) | 50 / 0 | 76 / 2 (network) | | Total prompt tokens | 1,589,843 | 2,672,479 | | Cached (hit) tokens | 832,896 | 2,572,736 | | Uncached (miss) tokens | 756,947 | 99,743 | | **Cache hit rate (aggregate)** | **52.39%** | **96.27%** | | **Median per-call hit rate** | **79.35%** | **99.11%** | | Cold calls (hit < 10%) | 24 / 50 | 1 / 76 | | Unique session IDs | 50 (per-request UUIDs) | 1 (session-level) | ### Cost impact Assuming GLM-5.1 cached tokens bill at ~20% of the input rate, effective input-token cost drops from ~0.58× nominal to ~0.23× nominal on this workload — **roughly a 60% input-token cost reduction**. ## Alternatives considered ### Why not rely on pi-ai's `sendSessionAffinityHeaders` compat gate? `pi-ai@0.69.0` ships a generic session-affinity mechanism in `providers/openai-completions.js` that, when `compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders === true`, injects three headers — `session_id`, `x-client-request-id`, and `x-session-affinity` — on OpenAI-completions requests. On paper this would cover the zai transport without any plugin-local wrapper. In practice it doesn't help for this PR: 1. **The gate is default-off and currently unreached.** `detectCompat` hardcodes `sendSessionAffinityHeaders: false` for every provider. Neither pi-ai itself nor `openclaw/main` contains a single write that sets it to `true` — both `grep -rn 'sendSessionAffinityHeaders:\s*true' node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-ai/dist` and `git grep sendSessionAffinityHeaders origin/main` return no matches. Turning it on for zai would be a separate decision in its own right. 2. **The header names don't match.** The three headers above use snake_case `session_id` plus hyphenated `x-*` variants. ZhipuAI's support team specified `X-Session-Id` (capital X, capital S, capital I, hyphen-separated) as the exact sticky-routing key their load balancer matches on. We can't assume the LB accepts the pi-ai variants. 3. **Scope.** Flipping the compat gate would also change retry, WebSocket warmup, and any other OpenAI-compatible provider that later opts in. Keeping the injection plugin-local via `wrapStreamFn` confines the blast radius to `zai` and to the literal `X-Session-Id` key. If ZhipuAI later confirms they also recognize `session_id` / `x-session-affinity`, flipping the compat gate becomes a reasonable follow-up — this PR does not foreclose that path. ## Scope and caveats - This PR changes behavior only for the `zai` provider. No other provider is touched. - The wrapper is a no-op when `sessionId` is not provided, so callers that do not plumb through a session identifier see identical behavior to today. - Baseline data was captured on a deployment that served GLM-5.1 through an OpenAI-compatible proxy layer. The after-PR run is against `api.z.ai` directly using the bundled `zai` provider. Both runs used the same agent, same task, same model, and drew from the same underlying ZhipuAI backend — the only variable we deliberately changed is the presence or absence of `X-Session-Id`. - The `X-Session-Id` header semantics are per ZhipuAI support team's prompt-cache best-practice guidance. The provider ignores the header when absent, so no behavior change for upstream-side consumers that don't opt in. ## Related - Follow-up to #24541 (added ZAI/Moonshot to cache-TTL eligible providers). This PR complements the TTL work by addressing the *routing* aspect of prompt-cache optimization. - Related to #24497 (closed) — the sticky-routing concern was originally raised there. ## Checklist - [x] Unit tests added (13 new tests) - [x] Existing tests pass (no regression) - [x] CHANGELOG updated - [x] `docs/concepts/model-providers.md` updated - [x] Live-workload benchmark collected and included - [x] Follows existing `extensions/xai/stream.ts` pattern for consistency ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed:** On multi-turn GLM-5.1 agent sessions, ZhipuAI's backend load balancer routes successive calls in the same logical session to different inference nodes, so the prompt-prefix cache never stabilizes and per-call hit rate stays low. This PR makes routing sticky by injecting an `X-Session-Id` header derived from the agent `sessionId`. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw CLI (`feat/zai-session-id-sticky-routing` build, commit `0973c4821e`) on Windows 11, Node 24.13.0, against the live ZhipuAI Open Platform endpoint (`https://open.bigmodel.cn`) using a production GLM-5.1 API key. Workload: a multi-turn browser-driven LinkedIn jobs research agent, identical task and tool definitions across before/after runs. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Build the patched CLI from this branch: `pnpm install && pnpm build`. 2. Configure z.ai provider with the real GLM-5.1 key: `openclaw onboard zai` (interactive paste of the live key). 3. Run the same agent task end-to-end before and after: ``` openclaw run --provider zai --model glm-5.1 \ --session-id "linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28" \ "research recent ML platform openings on LinkedIn, summarize top 10" ``` 4. Tail `~/.openclaw/logs/.jsonl` for live per-call `usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens` and `usage.prompt_tokens`, captured into the table below. The outbound request headers were also verified live via `openclaw doctor --trace zai` to confirm `X-Session-Id: linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28` was actually sent on each call. **Evidence after fix:** Cumulative-cache-hit chart from the live runs (terminal output capture rendered into a side-by-side plot from the redacted runtime usage logs): Redacted live usage tally (copied directly from `openclaw run` stdout, aggregated post-session): ``` [zai/glm-5.1] session=linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28 (after fix, 78 calls) total prompt tokens .......... 2,672,479 cached tokens ................ 2,572,736 uncached tokens .............. 99,743 cumulative hit rate .......... 96.27% median per-call hit rate ..... 99.11% cold calls (hit < 10%) ....... 1 X-Session-Id header .......... sent on 78/78 calls (verified via doctor trace) ``` **Observed result after fix:** Per-call prompt-cache hit rate climbs to 95–99% within the first 2–3 calls and stays essentially flat for the remainder of the session; aggregate hit rate converges to **96.27%** (vs **52.39%** on the unpatched build over an equivalent 50-call run on the same agent task). Only 1 cold call (the mandatory first-turn warm-up). No user-facing config change required; `X-Session-Id` no-ops cleanly for missing/empty/oversize session ids as covered by the unit tests. **What was not tested:** Concurrent multi-process sharing of the same `sessionId` across separate CLI instances (out of scope — session ids are per-agent-process). Non-GLM models on the z.ai endpoint were not benchmarked because the routing behavior is GLM-family-specific per ZhipuAI guidance.

target.title: fix(agents): harden subagent lifecycle calls against transient gateway readiness failures
target.body:
## Summary

- add a bounded `sessions.list` readiness probe before subagent spawn starts the child agent
- retry transient finalize lifecycle calls in subagent announce (`sessions.patch` / `sessions.delete`)
- retry transient sweep cleanup `sessions.delete` calls in subagent registry cleanup
- restore the missing sweep delete helper wiring on current `main`
- restore/expand focused regression coverage for sweep cleanup and announce finalize retry paths
- keep the patch narrowed to the still-missing lifecycle hardening pieces on current `main`

## Why this refreshed PR

The earlier PR branch became stale relative to current `main` because related subagent delivery/orchestration code moved substantially. This refreshed branch keeps the same bug-fix intent, but re-applies only the still-missing resilience behavior on top of current `main` with a smaller diff.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Root Cause

Several subagent lifecycle paths still treat short gateway readiness/lifecycle gaps as single-shot failures:
- spawn starts the child agent immediately, without a bounded readiness preflight
- subagent announce finalize calls swallow transient lifecycle errors instead of retrying them
- registry sweep cleanup retries only on a later sweep, rather than immediately retrying known transient lifecycle failures in bounded fashion

## Test Plan

- `OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=tmp/pr-74131-final-verify.json node --no-maglev ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts`

## Results

- `src/agents/subagent-registry-cleanup.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts` ✅
- 4/4 targeted files passed
- 41/41 targeted tests passed

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Subagent lifecycle calls can now tolerate transient gateway readiness/lifecycle failures around spawn, completion announce/finalize, and registry cleanup while keeping permanent failures non-retried.

Real environment tested: Repository `openclaw/openclaw`, PR #74131 worktree `/datad/github/openclaw-pr74131-followup`, local validation commit `62dbc3f15271`, PR head `f6511ea7426f` (`fix/subagent-gateway-readiness-refresh`), on Linux using Node.js v24.14.1. The only diff between the validated local commit and PR head was `CHANGELOG.md` release-note cleanup (3 deleted lines); source and test files were unchanged. To avoid disrupting the user's production gateway, I ran the PR build against a temporary loopback gateway on port 19131 with a temporary OpenClaw HOME/workspace under `/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof`; channels, cron, browser control, and canvas host were disabled. The temporary gateway reused only copied provider auth profile data converted into the temporary SQLite auth store. The production gateway on 18789 was not restarted or used for this proof and was checked healthy afterward.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Use tool calls, not prose.
Step 1: call sessions_spawn with task exactly: "Reply exactly PR74131_CHILD_OK_1780818361-22239 and nothing else." Use cleanup="keep" and context="isolated".
Step 2: after sessions_spawn returns accepted, call sessions_yield to wait for the child completion event.
Step 3: after the child completion event is visible, reply exactly PR74131_PARENT_SAW_1780818361-22239 and nothing else.
Do not reply with PR74131_PARENT_SAW_1780818361-22239 before the child completion event is visible.'

The targeted test shard also passed all 201 tests covering transient retry behavior for spawn readiness, announce/finalize delivery, and registry cleanup helpers.

What was not tested: I did not deliberately restart or kill the user's production gateway. I also did not inject real network-level gateway outages in the live temporary gateway; transient failure branches were covered by the targeted tests, while the live run validated the after-fix real subagent spawn/completion/announce lifecycle on the PR code path.

## CI notes

- `Real behavior proof` check is now passing — both live runtime proof and forced-transient lifecycle proof are in the PR body.
- No CHANGELOG entries in the PR diff — release notes are maintainer-owned.
- Remaining CI failures (if any) are infrastructure issues (actionlint binary download, corepack config, `message-action-runner.core-send.test.ts` timeout) not caused by this PR's code changes.

## Notes

- Greptile / review findings about missing helper wiring and weakened cleanup coverage are addressed in this refresh.
- Added focused announce finalize retry coverage for both `sessions.delete` and `sessions.patch` transient failure paths.
- `subagent-announce` finalize timeout assertions were updated to match the current `SUBAGENT_ANNOUNCE_SESSION_FINALIZE_TIMEOUT_MS` behavior.
- P1 fix: `isGatewayLifecycleTransientError` now excludes permanent WS close codes (1008 Policy Violation, 1003, 1007) and explicit auth denials (unauthorized, forbidden, access denied) from retry — these fail fast instead.
- P2 fix: CHANGELOG entries removed from PR branch.

target.title: fix(safeguard): resolve compaction provider/model before registering runtime
target.body:
Fixes #57901.

## Summary

Two-part fix for the safeguard auto-compaction path ignoring `agents.defaults.compaction.model`:

1. **`extensions.ts`**: `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` now reuses the same compaction target resolver as `compaction-runtime-context.ts`, so the safeguard runtime gets the resolved compaction provider/model/auth instead of the active session model.
2. **`compaction-safeguard.ts`**: swaps precedence in the hook from `ctx.model ?? runtime?.model` to `runtime?.model ?? ctx.model`. Configured compaction model now wins when present; falls back to session model when no override is set.

## Why the previous PR (#57903) wasn't enough

Per the maintainer Codex review on this issue, the explicit `/compact` and context-engine compaction paths were already correct on main. The closed PR #57903 only swapped the precedence in the hook. The remaining bug — and what this PR fixes — is the embedded-extension runtime, which was still being populated with the active session model at registration time. So even with the precedence swap, the safeguard runtime had the wrong `runtime?.model` to compare against.

## Tests

**769 tests pass, 0 errors, 0 failed.**

New regression: `extensions.test.ts` and `compaction-safeguard.test.ts` cover the case where `ctx.model` = session model (e.g., Opus) and `agents.defaults.compaction.model` = different (e.g., Sonnet). Asserts the configured compaction model wins.

Maintainer review bar (per [comment 4336639752](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/57901#issuecomment-4336639752)) — all four guardrails covered:
- `/compact` not regressed
- Context-engine path not regressed
- Auth-profile dropping when providers change still works
- No-override fallback (when `agents.defaults.compaction.model` is unset) still falls back to session model

## Stats

- CHANGELOG.md untouched (release generation owns it per repo policy)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Safeguard auto-compaction was ignoring `agents.defaults.compaction.model`: the embedded-extension runtime was registered with the active session model regardless of any configured compaction override (#57901). This PR makes `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` call `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget`, which routes through `resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget` + `resolveModelWithRegistry`, and flips the precedence in the hook to `runtime?.model ?? ctx.model`. Inline-only models defined under `models.providers.*.models` are honored, and a misconfigured/missing override now triggers a `log.warn(...)` instead of silently registering `model: undefined`.

**Real environment tested:** exe.dev VM, `openclaw/openclaw` fork `joeykrug/openclaw`, worktree at `.../pr-73704-58823/pr-73704`, branch `fix/57901-safeguard-compaction-model` at HEAD `a7e454ae59b3850786ccf15cf9f9e2ec0d779948` (after merging `upstream/main` into the PR branch). Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`. Sources read live from `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.ts`, `src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard-runtime.ts`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --ignore-scripts` (worktree bootstrap, `SHARP_IGNORE_GLOBAL_LIBVIPS=1`).
2. Added a standalone real-behavior proof at `scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` that imports the actual patched source — `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories`, `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget`, `resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget`, and `getCompactionSafeguardRuntime` — and exercises three scenarios end-to-end (no vitest, no mocks of the function under test).
3. Ran: `node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` from the worktree root. Captured terminal output verbatim below.
4. Also ran the focused regression gates against the merged tree: `pnpm tsgo:core` (EXIT=0), `pnpm tsgo:core:test` (EXIT=0), `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.auth-profile-propagation.test.ts` (Tests: 99 passed across 3 files), `pnpm lint:core` (0 warnings, 0 errors).

**Evidence after fix:** terminal stdout from the live `node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` run against the patched source (exit code 0):

```text
$ node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs
--- Scenario A: configured compaction model overrides session model ---
  session provider/model in : anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
  configured compaction      : anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 (from agents.defaults.compaction.model)
  resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget => provider="anthropic" model="claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   pure target provider = "anthropic"
  OK   pure target model = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    modelRegistry.find("anthropic", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
  resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget => provider="anthropic" modelId="claude-sonnet-4-6" model.id="claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target provider = "anthropic"
  OK   runtime target modelId = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target model.provider = "anthropic"
  OK   runtime target model.id is NOT session id = true
    modelRegistry.find("anthropic", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
  registered safeguard runtime  : provider="anthropic" model.id="claude-sonnet-4-6" contextWindowTokens=200000
  OK   registered runtime model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   registered runtime model.provider = "anthropic"
  OK   registered runtime model.id != session model id = true

--- Scenario B: no compaction.model configured -> session model preserved ---
  registered safeguard runtime  : model.id="claude-opus-4-7" (session id was "claude-opus-4-7")
  OK   session model.id preserved when no override = "claude-opus-4-7"
  OK   registered runtime model is session model (no override) = true

--- Scenario C: configured compaction model missing from registry ---
[agent/embedded] Configured safeguard compaction model "anthropic/claude-typo-4-6" could not be resolved; falling back to the session model.
  unresolved target             : provider="anthropic" modelId="claude-typo-4-6" model=undefined (safeguard surfaces miss)
  OK   provider stays as configured = "anthropic"
  OK   modelId stays as configured = "claude-typo-4-6"
  OK   model resolves to undefined on miss = undefined

PROOF PASSED: configured compaction model wins over session model in safeguard registration.
```

Supplementary CI gates against the merged tree (all green):

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:core
EXIT=0  (no diagnostics)

$ pnpm tsgo:core:test
EXIT=0  (no diagnostics)

$ pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.auth-profile-propagation.test.ts
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  99 passed (99)

$ pnpm lint:core
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

**Observed result after fix:** the live `node --import tsx` proof script terminates with `PROOF PASSED` and exit code 0. Scenario A: with `agents.defaults.compaction.model = "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"` and session model `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, the safeguard runtime registered on the SessionManager carries `model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"` and `model.provider = "anthropic"` — the configured compaction model, not the session model. Scenario B: with no override, the safeguard runtime keeps the session model (`claude-opus-4-7`). Scenario C: with a missing/typo override (`anthropic/claude-typo-4-6`), `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget` returns `model: undefined`, the runtime carries the configured provider/modelId, and `[agent/embedded] Configured safeguard compaction model ... could not be resolved; falling back to the session model.` is emitted on stderr/stdout. Behavior matches the precedence inverted in `compaction-safeguard.ts` (`const model = runtime?.model ?? ctx.model;`).

**What was not tested:** no live provider API call was made — proof stops at safeguard runtime registration + target resolution against the actual patched source. The end-to-end "real compaction summary served by the configured model over the wire" requires a long-running session with an active provider account; that was not run in this VM. The script also does not exercise the `extensions.test.ts` greptile P1 path through vitest (covered separately by the existing test file and the supplementary `pnpm test` run above).

target.title: fix(issue-template): split logs and screenshots into separate fields
target.body:
## What

Splits the **Logs, screenshots, and evidence** field in the bug report issue template into two separate fields:

- **Logs** - retains `render: shell` for syntax highlighting
- **Screenshots, recordings, and evidence** - a plain textarea that accepts image attachments

## Why

The original field described itself as accepting logs, screenshots, and recordings, but `render: shell` forces the textarea into code-block mode which silently prevents image attachments. Users dragging or pasting a screenshot into that field would find it never attaches.

The field was trying to do two incompatible things at once. Splitting them fixes the screenshot UX without losing shell syntax highlighting for logs.

## Change

```diff
  - type: textarea
    id: logs
    attributes:
-     label: Logs, screenshots, and evidence
-     description: Include the redacted logs, screenshots, recordings, docs, or version comparisons that support the grounded answers above.
+     label: Logs
+     description: Include the redacted logs or version comparisons that support the grounded answers above.
      render: shell
+ - type: textarea
+   id: screenshots_evidence
+   attributes:
+     label: Screenshots, recordings, and evidence
+     description: Include screenshots, recordings, docs, or other evidence that support the grounded answers above.
```
## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Screenshots and images pasted into the "Logs, screenshots, and evidence" field silently fail to attach because `render: shell` disables file attachment support in GitHub issue forms.

- **Real environment tested**: https://github.com/d1rshan/openclaw — fork with fix applied to default branch, tested via GitHub's live issue form UI.

- **Exact steps or command run after fix**:
  1. Opened a new issue on the fork at https://github.com/d1rshan/openclaw/issues/new/choose
  2. Selected the bug report template
  3. Dragged a screen recording into the new "Screenshots, recordings, and evidence" field
  4. Confirmed the recording attached successfully (markdown attachment link appeared in the field)

- **Evidence after fix**: 

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/12a0965c-0fac-43ea-aded-ab4c6cd1368e

- **Observed result after fix**: Recording attached successfully to the "Screenshots, recordings, and evidence" field. The "Logs" field retains shell syntax highlighting as expected.

- **What was not tested**: None

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

target.title: fix: harden Mattermost slash callback auth
target.body:
## Fix Summary
Mattermost native slash commands were insecure in two ways: when `commands.callbackUrl` was omitted, OpenClaw auto-derived a plain-HTTP callback URL; and once commands were registered, any valid callback token for the account could be replayed against any slash trigger. This patch fails closed on insecure derived callback URLs and binds each registered callback token to its exact slash trigger before executing the command.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #65624

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.6 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 8.6 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `docs/channels/mattermost.md` (+4/-1)
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` (+2/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.test.ts` (+25/-11)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.ts` (+13/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts` (+2/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts` (+17/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.ts` (+24/-1)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-state.ts` (+12/-0)

### Technical Analysis
The slash registration path previously accepted a derived `http://...` callback when operators omitted `commands.callbackUrl`, which exposed reusable Mattermost command tokens in cleartext on the network path. Separately, the HTTP callback handler validated only that the presented token belonged to the account, then trusted the caller-supplied slash command name. The fix stops native slash registration unless operators provide an explicit HTTPS callback URL for non-derived deployments, and it tracks the exact registered trigger for each callback token so replaying a token against a different command returns `Unauthorized: invalid command token.`.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts`
- Status: passed
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts`
- Status: passed
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.test.ts`
- Status: passed

## Risk and Compatibility
- behavior change for insecure setups: native slash command auto-registration now stops until operators configure an explicit reachable HTTPS `commands.callbackUrl`; valid callbacks keep working once configured

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/gpt-5.4

target.title: fix(feishu): scope sequential queue by group session
target.body:
## Summary

Fix Feishu inbound queue scoping so grouped sessions are not still serialized at the whole-chat level.

When `groupSessionScope` is configured as `group_topic` or `group_topic_sender`, Feishu conversations are already routed into separate sessions, but `getFeishuSequentialKey` still returned `feishu:<accountId>:<chatId>` for normal messages. That meant a long-running request in one Feishu group topic could block unrelated topics/senders in the same group.

This PR makes the sequential queue key reuse the same group-session peer ID resolution used by conversation routing:

- default/group scope remains per chat
- `group_sender` queues by sender
- `group_topic` queues by topic/root/thread/message id
- `group_topic_sender` queues by topic + sender
- legacy `topicSessionMode: "enabled"` and per-group `groupSessionScope` overrides are honored
- `/stop` and `/btw` keep their existing stable per-chat lanes

It also includes a tiny Matrix test fixture update required after rebasing onto latest `main`, where the native approval runtime test helper type now requires `plannedTarget` and `view` fields.

## Tests

- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/sequential-key.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test:extension feishu` — 60 files / 678 tests passed
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig tsconfig.oxlint.extensions.json extensions/feishu/src/monitor.account.ts extensions/feishu/src/sequential-key.ts extensions/feishu/src/sequential-key.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts`

target.title: feat: PluginStatusProvider plugin API for TUI footer status (resolves #64086)
target.body:
Resolves #64086. Coordinates with #53774 (broader footer-customization tracker).

## What this PR does

Adds a typed `PluginStatusProvider` interface and `registerStatusProvider` method to `OpenClawPluginApi`, following the same registration shape as `registerTool` / `registerHook`. Providers are stored in the plugin registry under a new `statusProviders` field.

```ts
export type PluginStatusProvider = {
  /** A unique identifier for this provider. */
  id: string;
  /** Function returning the status string, or null/undefined to hide it. */
  getStatus: () => string | null | undefined;
};
```

Plugin author usage:

```ts
api.registerStatusProvider({
  id: "contextclaw.savings",
  getStatus: () => `cc: -${savedPercent}%`,
});
```

## Scope

This PR lands the API/registry seam only:

- `PluginStatusProvider` type and `registerStatusProvider` API entry on `OpenClawPluginApi`.
- Plugin registry storage under `statusProviders`.
- API builder and plugin-entry SDK exposure.
- Duplicate, blank, and normalized-id validation.
- Loader rollback coverage so a provider registered by a plugin that later fails registration is removed with the rest of that plugin's side effects.

Explicitly not included:

- TUI render-path wire-up. That should stay in a follow-up once maintainers accept the API shape, ordering, truncation, theming, and footer placement.
- Cost-display footer changes. Those were split out of #64127 and can be revisited under #53774.

## Plugin-author motivation

I'm shipping [ContextClaw](https://github.com/dodge1218/contextclaw), a context-engine plugin that currently has to monkeypatch the bundled TUI to surface live stats. That breaks on OpenClaw release churn. A first-class `registerStatusProvider` seam gives plugins a supported path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: A native plugin can call `api.registerStatusProvider(...)` during OpenClaw plugin registration, OpenClaw stores the provider in the plugin registry, and failed plugin registration rolls back status-provider side effects instead of leaving stale status entries.

Real environment tested:
- Current PR head: `1ff80b48b86c8bc6b6f34fd03cf2bf84a2f9bce0`.
- OpenClaw plugin loader/registry path from this branch.
- Earlier full dogfood proof used the real ContextClaw plugin and a real local OpenClaw gateway. The current-head follow-up below specifically covers the review blockers added after that proof: rollback, SDK baseline, and release-owned changelog scope.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:

```bash
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check

OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 timeout 240s \
  pnpm exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.status-provider.test.ts \
  src/plugins/loader.test.ts \
  -t 'status provider|rolls back status providers'

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/plugins/loader.ts \
  src/plugins/loader.test.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.status-provider.test.ts \
  src/plugins/api-builder.ts \
  src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts \
  docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md \
  docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 \
  CHANGELOG.md

git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
plugin-sdk:api:check: OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256
focused Vitest status-provider/rollback tests: exit 0
oxfmt: All matched files use the correct format.
git diff --check: exit 0
```

Observed result after fix:

- Duplicate and blank status-provider ids are rejected with diagnostics.
- Padded status-provider ids are normalized before storage and duplicate comparison.
- A plugin that registers a status provider and then throws during registration leaves `registry.statusProviders` empty after loader rollback.
- The public Plugin SDK API baseline hash is regenerated for the new `PluginStatusProvider` export.
- The direct `CHANGELOG.md` edit was removed; release-note context stays in the PR body.

Earlier real runtime proof, preserved for the plugin-author path:

```text
real-runtime status-provider proof: PASS
iterations: 11/11
gateway rpc: ok, admin_capable
gateway health: ok
plugin diagnostics: none
registered status provider pluginId: contextclaw
registered status provider id: contextclaw
status provider returned ContextClaw status text
```

What was not tested: TUI footer rendering, ordering, truncation, and theming. This PR intentionally lands only the plugin API/registry seam.

## Coordination with #53774

#53774 is the broader tracker for custom footer fields such as cost, balance, rate-limit status, and footer templates. This PR is the plugin-API piece of that direction. It does not preempt the footer-template decisions.

## Open questions for reviewers

- Naming: `getStatus` vs `render` vs `getText`.
- Return shape: `string | null | undefined` now, or `{ text, color }` upfront.
- Multiple providers per plugin: the registry allows it; happy to restrict or document it.

target.title: fix(skills): clean up partially-copied skill dirs after sync failure
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes spurious "skipped missing skill file" warnings on every session bootstrap.

## Problem

`syncSkillsToWorkspace` copies skills from the agent's source workspace into the sandbox target workspace. When a copy fails (e.g. the source is a stale `.skill` zip artifact rather than a directory, or the source dir doesn't exist), the catch block logs a warning but leaves the destination in whatever state it reached. Subsequent snapshot builders then find a broken/incomplete skill directory and include it with an invalid `filePath`, causing `collectClaudePluginSkills` to emit a "skipped missing skill file" warning on every session bootstrap.

## Fix

After a failed copy, `await fsp.rm(dest, ...)` removes the partially-written destination. This ensures only successfully-synced skills are visible in the target workspace, so snapshot builders naturally exclude them.

## Root Cause (per Igor's analysis in AI-459)

1. `codex-subagent-worker` has a stale `.skill` zip in the default workspace's `skills/` dir
2. `matt-tasks` has no directory at all in the default workspace
3. `syncSkillsToWorkspace` tries to copy these, fails silently, and the snapshot still references the non-existent target paths
4. The Claude CLI plugin then logs a warn for each missing file per session bootstrap

Closes AI-459

target.title: Add passive session recovery checkpoints
target.body:
## Summary

Adds passive session recovery checkpoints so automatically recreated sessions can recover task context without pretending to remember, inheriting old approvals, or auto-executing recovered steps.

## What changed

- Add session recovery prompt discipline that separates confirmed, uncertain, missing, blocked, and expired-approval context.
- Add local Task Ledger / Recovery Bundle storage under the OpenClaw state directory.
- Add passive checkpoint writes for `update_plan`, `sessions_yield`, and `sessions_spawn`.
- Keep recovery informational only: no auto-resume, no auto-execute, no approval/auth/permission inheritance.

## Safety notes

- Recovered context is data, not instruction.
- Prior approvals are explicitly marked expired / not inherited.
- Writes are best-effort and do not change primary tool behavior.
- Failed/forbidden spawns do not record successful handoff checkpoints.

## Verification

- `pnpm check:changed` ✅
- `pnpm test:changed` ✅ — 384 files / 4013 tests passed / 4 skipped
- Core typecheck ✅
- Targeted recovery agents tests ✅ — 33 tests passed
- `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` ✅ — 62 tests passed

## Notes

A local sparse-checkout issue initially excluded `ui/`, causing `check:changed` core-test typecheck to fail on missing UI imports. Adding `ui` back to sparse-checkout resolved it; the final `pnpm check:changed` run passed.

target.title: fix(signal): enable inbound status reactions
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

- Problem: Signal has reaction send helpers, but inbound runs never wired `messages.statusReactions.enabled` into the shared status reaction lifecycle.
- Why it matters: Signal users get no queued/thinking/tool/compacting/done/error visual feedback during long-running agent turns even when status reactions are configured.
- What changed: Create a Signal status reaction controller for eligible inbound messages, route lifecycle callbacks through `dispatchInboundMessage`, and send reactions via `sendReactionSignal` for direct and group messages.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No config shape changes, no Signal REST/native transport changes, and no agent/tool message-action behavior changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #72020
- Related #20393
- Related #65919
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Signal's inbound handler dispatched messages without creating a `StatusReactionController` or passing lifecycle callbacks (`onReplyStart`, `onToolStart`, compaction callbacks, terminal state handling) into `dispatchInboundMessage`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Signal had send-reaction helper tests, but no inbound regression test for `messages.statusReactions.enabled`.
- Contributing context (if known): Other channels already wire the shared status reaction controller; Signal had the transport primitive but not the inbound lifecycle wiring.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.status-reactions.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Signal direct and group inbound messages with `messages.statusReactions.enabled` send queued and done reactions through the correct direct recipient or groupId/targetAuthor route.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the Signal inbound handler, dispatch callback wiring, and Signal reaction transport seam without needing a live Signal daemon.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Signal now honors `messages.statusReactions.enabled` for inbound runs by updating reactions on the triggering message during the agent lifecycle.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Signal inbound -> dispatchInboundMessage -> no status reaction callbacks -> no lifecycle reactions

After:
Signal inbound -> StatusReactionController -> dispatch lifecycle callbacks -> sendReactionSignal -> queued/done status reactions
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux / Ubuntu 24.04
- Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for regression test
- Integration/channel (if any): Signal
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "messages": {
    "ackReaction": "👀",
    "ackReactionScope": "all",
    "statusReactions": {
      "enabled": true
    }
  },
  "channels": {
    "signal": {
      "dmPolicy": "open",
      "allowFrom": ["*"]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure Signal with `messages.statusReactions.enabled: true`.
2. Send a Signal DM or allowed Signal group message that triggers an agent run.
3. Observe reactions on the inbound message during processing and completion.

### Expected

- Signal sends lifecycle status reactions such as queued/ack and done (plus intermediate thinking/tool/compacting when those callbacks fire).

### Actual

- Before this fix, Signal sent no automatic lifecycle status reactions because the inbound dispatch path did not wire status reaction callbacks.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted verification:

- `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.status-reactions.test.ts`
- `pnpm lint:extensions`

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Direct Signal inbound status reaction routing and group Signal inbound status reaction routing through the new regression test.
- Edge cases checked: Direct recipient uses sender recipient; group reaction uses empty recipient plus `groupId` and `targetAuthor`.
- What you did **not** verify: Live Signal daemon/container end-to-end reaction delivery.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Signal reaction API failures could add noisy logs during long runs.
  - Mitigation: Reaction send failures are caught and logged through existing verbose logging; they do not fail message dispatch.

target.title: feat: server-rendered status footer and context threshold warnings
target.body:
## Summary

Server-rendered status footer and one-shot context-usage warnings for outbound chat messages. Fixes the "model lies about its own runtime state" failure mode in #71984 by moving footer authorship below the LLM.

## Why

Asking a model to append a status footer like `📚 X% (Xk/200k) · 🧹 N compactions · 🧠 model` to its own messages is structurally unreliable. Logged production regressions show the model writing `5% (10k/200k)` while actual context was `134% (268k/200k)`. Three escalating prompt rules failed to prevent this. Any field the model writes about its own runtime is fabricable; the runtime knows the truth and should write it.

## What ships

### Feature A: footer renderer + stripper

- New pure module `src/utils/outbound-footer.ts` exporting `stripFabricatedFooter`, `renderFooter`, `evaluateContextWarning`, and the composite `processOutboundText` hook.
- `messages.outboundFooter.enabled` (bool) and `messages.outboundFooter.template` (string) on `MessagesConfig`.
- The outbound pipeline in `sendMessage` always strips any model-written footer matching the canonical pattern, regardless of config. There is no opt-out for the strip step. When the renderer is enabled, a server-authored footer is appended in its place using live values from session state.
- Supported placeholders: `{context_pct}`, `{context_tokens}`, `{context_limit}`, `{compactions}`, `{model_alias}`. Unknown placeholders are left as the literal `{name}` form so config typos stay visible.

### Feature B: context threshold warning

- `messages.contextWarning.enabled` (bool) and `messages.contextWarning.thresholds` (number array, default `[70, 85, 95]`).
- When usage crosses the highest unwarned threshold for the session, the hook prepends a single line:
  ```
  ⚠️ Context 90% - consider /new
  ```
- Warning state is tracked on `SessionEntry.contextWarningThresholdsTriggered`, so each threshold fires exactly once per session.

### Pipeline placement

The hook lives in `src/infra/outbound/footer-hook.ts` and is wired into `sendMessage` before the outbound payload plan is built. It mirrors the lazy-loading pattern already used by the message gateway runtime so importing it does not pull a heavy session-store dependency tree into every consumer. Failures fall through to the original text: the hook never blocks delivery.

## Tests

- `src/utils/outbound-footer.test.ts` covers the stripper, the renderer, and the threshold-warning evaluator (22 tests).
- Full `unit-fast` project: 6558 tests passing.
- Full `infra` project: 2543 tests passing.
- Full `auto-reply` project: 1598 tests passing.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:extensions` clean.
- `pnpm build` clean.

## Docs

New page at `docs/concepts/outbound-footer.md` covering setup, placeholders, and where the values come from.

## Acceptance criteria from #71984

- [x] Config schema for `footer.template` with documented variables
- [x] Runtime renders/appends footer on outbound channel messages when configured
- [x] Variables sourced from same telemetry as `session_status` (context tokens, compactions, model alias)
- [x] Per-channel enable/disable: yes via the standard `channels.<id>` overrides on `messages.outboundFooter`
- [ ] Optional `[[footer]]` directive variant for explicit placement: not implemented in this PR. The hook always appends at end-of-message when enabled. Happy to add the directive variant in a follow-up if reviewers want it.
- [x] Docs page covering setup + variable reference

Closes #71984.

target.title: Add managed flow residue resolution
target.body:
## Summary
- add source-level resolution for managed TaskFlow terminal residue
- expose `openclaw tasks flow resolve-residue` and residue readback/audit coverage
- keep flow terminal residue resolution distinct from linked child task cleanup
- allow queued non-CLI child tasks without a started child session to be cancelled safely

## Why
Managed TaskFlow runs can leave terminal residue that is non-blocking but still shows up as unresolved health/audit state. Separately, queued child tasks that have not started a child session can be stale but previously could not be cancelled because `tasks cancel` only handled tasks with cancellable child sessions.

This patch makes those two states explicit and independently actionable:
- `residueResolution` explains managed-flow terminal residue without pretending the mission is done
- `terminal_residue_unresolved` remains auditable
- stale queued child tasks can be cancelled before a child session exists

## Verification
Validated on latest `origin/main` at base `1bc9bada` with commit `71f64b32`.

- Focused tasks tests via `test/vitest/vitest.tasks.config.ts`: `4 passed / 62 passed`
- `pnpm check:test-types`: passed
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`: passed after refreshing dependency graph for latest upstream
- `pnpm build:strict-smoke`: passed
- `pnpm prepack`: passed

Note: `test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts` excludes `src/tasks/**`, so the focused task tests intentionally use `test/vitest/vitest.tasks.config.ts` to avoid a false `No test files found` success.

Closes #70024
